title: NMR and Conductivity Study of Polymer Electrolytes in the Imide Family: P(EO)/Li[N(SO2CnF2n+1)(SO2CmF2m+1)]
abstract:
620
¹ WILEY-VCH-Verlag GmbH, 69451 Weinheim, Germany, 2002
1439-4235/02/03/07 $ 20.00+.50/0
CHEMPHYSCHEM 2002, No. 7
NMR and Conductivity Study
of Polymer Electrolytes
in the Imide Family:
P(EO)/Li[N(SO2CnF2n1)(SO2CmF2m1)]
Wladimir Gorecki,[b] Christel Roux,[b]
Martin Cle¬mancey,[b] Michel Armand,*[a] and
Elie Belorizky[b]
KEYWORDS:
anions ¥ NMR spectroscopy ¥ polymer electrolytes ¥ sulfon-
imides
Polymer solid electrolytes formed by the dissolution of metal
salts in a polyether matrix have drawn sheer interest from the
scientific community.[1] This curiosity stems from both a theo-
retical point of view with a puzzling conduction mechanism[2±4]
and the perspective of important technological applications in
electrochemical devices, such as high energy density lithium
batteries, electrochromic devices, supercapacitors, and antistatic
coatings. Poly(ethylene oxide), (CH2CH2O)n, (PEO) has been the
object of the majority of the studies.
Due to its stereoregularity and symmetry, pristine PEO is a
semi-crystalline polymer with a melting point of 65C. The
stoichiometric complexes with salts of sufficiently low lattice
energy[5] are usually formed from three repeat units (EO) (3:1) per
salt and interact through the cations. In addition, for salts with
the lowest lattice energy, a more dilute, lower-melting 6:1
complex is known, as well as 2:1 and 1:1 compositions. The
existence of the 6:1 complex indicates higher conductivities. In
similarity to aqueous and other salt solutions, PEO and its
complexes form eutectics,[6] which are amorphous elastomers
above the liquidus point. Since it was shown by NMR that
conduction is restricted to the amorphous phase[7] of the
poly(ethylene oxide), this is a serious limiting factor for the
conductivity at room temperature. The strategies, which can be
combined to overcome the problem of low conductivity at
temperatures below the melting point of the eutectic in PEO
have been two-pronged: 1) modification of the polymer to
hinder crystallization through either copolymerization or struc-
tural changes such as comb branches; 2) design of new solutes
(salts) which feature favorable phase diagrams with PEO
(™plasticizing salts∫),[8] and promote a high degree of dissociation
of the opposite charges in this medium of low dielectric
constant.
The ionic conductors obtained by dissolution of lithium
trifluoromethanesulphonylimide in poly(ethylene oxide), (PEO)/
Li[N(SO2CF3)2], or other derived polymer solvents are now
commonly used in electrochemical devices as their conductivity
outperforms that obtained with any other salts. The phase
diagram is very favorable[8, 9, 10] with a eutectic at 1/9 Li/EO
lying close to room temperature. Interestingly, the speciation of
this salt in PEO has shown it to be devoid of contact ion pairs for
Li/EO  1/6, as shown by Raman spectroscopy.[11, 12] A pulsed field
gradient NMR study of this electrolyte for various compositions
had been undertaken[13] to show that the ratio of the measured
conductivity and the value deduced from the NMR diffusion
coefficient is close to unity. Hence, the use of the Nernst ± Ein-
stein equation is legitimate. This adds an indirect proof to the
notion of total dissociation or at least of a mechanism where
dissociation is a necessary step for nuclei motion. In all cases, and
as for salt solutions in liquids,[14] the mobility of the anions is
larger than that for cations, the transference number of the latter
being close to 0.3.
Without the complications inherent to incomplete dissocia-
tion, it was interesting to study the influence of the anion size on
transport properties, namely the conductivity and the diffusion
coefficients. Homologues in the perfluorosulfonimide series
have the same type of negative charge delocalization and
indeed the measure of their gas phase acidity (in the H form)
shows them to be even slightly stronger acids than trifluor-
omethyl sulfonamide (TFSI), a feature attributed to increased
electron-withdrawing power of longer perfluoroalkyl chains.[15]
Thus, the Li derivatives are expected to be comparable in
dissociation to LiTFSI. In this work, we have focused on the
systems P(EO)/{Li[N(SO2CnF2n1)(SO2CmF2m1)]}x where n1, m4
at the salt concentration expressed as a concentration ratio x
Li/EO1/8 and 1/16 and for the salts with nm1, 2, 3, 4; in
addition, the unsymmetrical salt n1, m4 was included in the
[11] H. Tada, K. Teranishi, S. Ito, H. Kobayashi, S. Kitagawa, Langmuir 2000, 16,
6077.
[12] D. A. Stern, E. Wellner, G. N. Salaita, L. Laguren-Davison, N. Batina, D. G.
Frank, D. C. Walton, A. T. Hubbard, J. Am. Chem. Soc. 1988, 110, 4885.
[13] M. A. Bryant, S. L. Joa, J. E. Pemberton, Langmuir 1992, 8, 753.
[14] J. Y. Gui, F. Lu, D. A. Stern, A. T. Hubbard, J. Electroanal. Chem. 1990, 292,
245.
[15] The n(Au) constituting a single Auc unit (about 470 atoms) multiplied by
the dispersion yielded n(surface Au atoms)  120. For cubo-octahedral
packing, the dispersion is approximately 0.25 at r 1.5 nm: G. A.
Somorjai, Introduction to Surface Chemistry and Catalysis, Wiley, New
York, NY, 1994, p. 10.
[16] P. M. W. Gill, B. G. Johnson, J. A. Pople, Int. J. Quantum Chem. Symp. 1992,
26, 319.

Laboratoire de Spectrome¬trie Physique
Universite¬ Joseph Fourier Grenoble I
BP 87, 38042 Saint-Martin-d'He¡res (France)
[] Present address:
Centre d'Etudes et de Recherches sur les Mate¬riaux
Commissariat a¡ l'E¬nergie Atomique
38054 Grenoble Cedex 9 (France)
 CHEMPHYSCHEM 2002, No. 7
¹ WILEY-VCH-Verlag GmbH, 69451 Weinheim, Germany, 2002
1439-4235/02/03/07 $ 20.00+.50/0
621
study to assess the influence of a
different spatial arrangement of
the CF2 moieties. These salts will
be denoted 1,1, 2,2, 3,3 ,
4,4, and 1,4, respectively. We
have measured the diffusion coef-
ficient of the lithium and fluorine
ions versus temperature. The results were analyzed in terms of
transport numbers of the lithium ions.
However, when dealing with the fluorine diffusion coefficient
measurements in the higher imide compounds, the pulsed
magnetic field gradient (PMFG) technique[16] appeared not
appropriate because the fluorine T2 relaxation times were too
short. Consequently, we used the simulated-echo technique
described by Hahn[17] and Tanner.[18]
Arrhenius plots of the temperature dependence of the ionic
conductivity for concentrations x  1/8 and 1/16 for the systems
PEO 1,1  , 2,2 , 3,3  , 4,4 , and 1,4  are shown in Figures 1
to 3. These measurements were performed during a decreasing
(Figure 1 and Figure 2) and an increasing (Figure 3) temperature
ramp. In Figure 1, for x  1/8, the typical curvature of the plots
indicates an amorphous phase (VFT or WLF) regime in the
Figure 1. Arrhenius plot of the conductivity of 2,2  , 3,3 , 4,4  , and 1,4 . The
concentration is x 1/8. Data have been collected during a cooling process.
Figure 2. Arrhenius plot of the conductivity of 1,1 , 2,2 , 3,3 , 4,4 , and
1,4 . The concentration is x 1/16. Data have been collected during a cooling
process.
Figure 3. Arrhenius plot of the conductivity of 1,1 , 2,2 , 3,3 , 4,4 , and
1,4 . The concentration is x 1/16. Data have been collected during a heating
process.
whole temperature range. This is in agreement with the
existence of the eutectic for the large anions close to this
concentration[9] between the leanest stoichiometric compound
known (x  1/6) and pure PEO. Although no attempt has been
made to determine the phase diagrams, the similarity in anion
structure suggests the location of the eutectic between x  1/8
and 1/9. As a rule, the eutectic point is found where supercooling
is more likely to be observed; thus, an elastomeric phase is
preserved throughout the explored temperature range and time
domain (several hours to days) even if the eutectic point is above
room temperature. The homologues of TFSI become less
conductive with increasing chain length, in the order 1,1 
 2,21,4  3,3 4,4  . The marked difference between
3,3  and 4,4  is quite interesting as it may indicate the
formation of aggregates; the very large repulsive energy of
perfluoroalkyl chains sets the threshold for micelle-type forma-
tion at low n m values (6 in water). Also to be taken into
consideration is the relative stiffness of the perfluoroalkyl chains
due to steric crowding of the adjacent fluorine atoms restricting
locally the segmental motion.
The same comments hold for the results presented in Figure 2
(x  1/16). This composition usually corresponds to the max-
imum conductivity above the liquidus temperature for PEO; but
also at any temperature when long-range order is suppressed in
the host polymer by introducing ™crystallinity breakers∫like
comonomers or by using a different polymer architecture (comb
branch). The knee observed at T330 K corresponds to the
recrystallization process of pure PEO[13] according to the
expected phase diagram. Here, even more apparent than for
x  1/8, compound 1,4  is very close in conductivity to 2,2  to
show that a single long perfluoroalkyl chain does not suffice to
form aggregates as in 4,4  . Indeed, 1,4  behaves like an n 
m  2.5 average-length chain. This is quite interesting in terms of
application, as 1,4  has the advantage of a high aluminum
corrosion potential, superior in this respect to TFSI and 2,2  .
Indeed, the depassivation of aluminum current collectors has up
to now slowed the entry of LiTFSI in the battery market despite
its excellent safety and stability characteristics in contrast to
currently used LiPF6. In this respect, 1,4  may provide an

ficient of the lithium and fluorine

short. Consequently, we used the simulated-echo technique
described by Hahn[17] and Tanner.[18]

existence of the eutectic for the large anions close to this
concentration[9] between the leanest stoichiometric compound
known (x  1/6) and pure PEO. Although no attempt has been
made to determine the phase diagrams, the similarity in anion
structure suggests the location of the eutectic between x  1/8
and 1/9. As a rule, the eutectic point is found where supercooling
is more likely to be observed; thus, an elastomeric phase is
preserved throughout the explored temperature range and time
domain (several hours to days) even if the eutectic point is above

conductive with increasing chain length, in the order 1,1 

The same comments hold for the results presented in Figure 2
(x  1/16). This composition usually corresponds to the max-
imum conductivity above the liquidus temperature for PEO; but
also at any temperature when long-range order is suppressed in
the host polymer by introducing ™crystallinity breakers∫like
comonomers or by using a different polymer architecture (comb
branch). The knee observed at T330 K corresponds to the
recrystallization process of pure PEO[13] according to the
expected phase diagram. Here, even more apparent than for
x  1/8, compound 1,4  is very close in conductivity to 2,2  to
show that a single long perfluoroalkyl chain does not suffice to
form aggregates as in 4,4  . Indeed, 1,4  behaves like an n 
m  2.5 average-length chain. This is quite interesting in terms of
application, as 1,4  has the advantage of a high aluminum
corrosion potential, superior in this respect to TFSI and 2,2  .
Indeed, the depassivation of aluminum current collectors has up
to now slowed the entry of LiTFSI in the battery market despite
its excellent safety and stability characteristics in contrast to
currently used LiPF6. In this respect, 1,4  may provide an
¹ WILEY-VCH-Verlag GmbH, 69451 Weinheim, Germany, 2002
1439-4235/02/03/07 $ 20.00+.50/0
interesting alternative. The existence of a eutectic for all salts is
demonstrated by the higher melting point, in the series of
compositions tested, found for pristine PEO.
In the higher imide, we used the simulated-echo techni-
que[17, 18] with the advantage to have a T1 dependence of the
relaxation. The pulse sequence is (/2)x (/2)y (/2)y and the
magnetic-field gradient pulses are applied immediately after the
first and the third RF pulses. We observed that the 19F spin echo
for compounds 3,3  and 4,4  and the shape of the fluorine
echo becomes more complex with increasing length of the
anion. Besides, the maximum of the echo amplitude is not very
well defined. Indeed, both the CF3 end groups and the catenary
CF2 moieties contribute to the signal. To separate the two, we
carried out a Fourier transform of the echo signal (Figure 4) and
Figure 4. Fourier transform of the fluorine spin echo for compound 4,4 with
separate contributions from terminal CF3 and catenary CF2 moieties.
deduced the area which corresponds to each curve. This
protocol was applied for each value of the magnetic gradient
pulse. We verified that all mobile fluorine nuclei had the same
diffusion coefficient, as expected for a chemically bonded
molecule. The short T2 value observed with longer perfluoroalkyl
chains, as in 4,4  is probably due to a stiffening of the polymer
complex due to the steric hindrance within the perfluoroalkyl
chains as in PTFE and is deleterious to the ionic conductivity. Any
aggregation of the perfluoroalkyl moieties would also contribute
to rigidify the system at the microscopic level.
Knowledge of the diffusion coefficients is necessary to
determine the individual carriers' contributions to the conduc-
tivity. We denote by 19D and 7D the anionic and cationic diffusion
coefficients, respectively, which were determined for the differ-
ent salts at various concentrations (1/8, 1/16) and temperatures
in the range T 312 ± 400 K. The results are shown in Figures 5a
(19F) and 5b (7Li) for x 1/8, in Figures 6a and 6b for x 1/16. In
Arrhenius coordinates, the temperature dependence of anionic
and cationic diffusion coefficients is closer to linearity than to the
curvature expected from a free-volume law with Tg 40C.
The apparent activation energy is the same for all samples within
experimental precision. The same feature is observed for
P(EO)LiTFSIx,[13] PPO(LiTFSI)x,[19] and P(EO)(LiCnF2n1SO3)x (1n8)
Figure 5. Temperature dependence of the anionic diffusion coefficient a) 19D and
b) 7D in 1,1 , 2,2 , 4,4  , and 1,4  at x 1/8. Data have been collected during
Figure 6. Temperature dependence of the anionic diffusion coefficient a) 19D and
b) 7D in 1,1  , 2,2 , 3,3 , and 1,4 at x 1/16. Data have been collected
¹ WILEY-VCH-Verlag GmbH, 69451 Weinheim, Germany, 2002
1439-4235/02/03/07 $ 20.00+.50/0
regime (Arrhenius or free volume) depending on concentration
as observed by Vincent et al. for P(EO)(LiPF6)x.[21] Consequently,
the activation energy does not depend on the nature of the
anion or its size. Conversely, the conductivity depends very much
on the chemical nature of the charge-carrying center. It would be
interesting to carry out experiments with the same anion but
different alkali ions. Rubidium salts are considered for further
studies because Rb is an efficient NMR nucleus may show larger
coordination numbers than Li. The salts examined here follow
the general rule, applicable to both aprotic liquid electrolytes
and solvating polymers, that the diffusion constant 19D of species
contained in the anion is larger than 7D at any temperature and
 x	 values investigated. This characteristic is customarily mis-
interpreted as a consequence of the strong cation solvation by
the favorably arranged EO segments. The fact that the same
phenomenon occurs in liquids (such as propylene carbonate,
one of the most studied nonprotogenic solvents[14]) points
towards a coupling of the motion of charges of opposite sign as
a general phenomenon. All anions investigated are markedly
larger than Li, thus Stokes' law does not apply, especially if we
consider that in a polymer, Li moves without any solvation
sphere (™immobile solvent∫). In the more dilute x 1/16 solution
and in the elastomeric phase, both
larger than the corresponding coefficients for x 1/8 at any
given temperature. Of course, and this is a general feature, the
larger the salt concentration, the less movement of the chain is
allowed, which leads to increased glass transition temperatures.
Finally, the most surprising result is that 19D and 7D values of the
sample with the unsymmetrical anion 1,4  are not markedly
different from to those corresponding to 1,1 .
NMR is insensitive to the charge of the diffusing nuclei. If we
assume complete dissociation (the absence of contact ion pairs
in these systems), as demonstrated by Rey et al.[11] for TFSI (1,1),
we obtain Equation (1), where T is the cation transference
number (the algebraic sum of the currents carried by all the Li-
containing species, such as Li2X Li, LiX2, etc.); t is the cation
transport number, assuming solely the existence of Li and X
species; and Li and X are the partial conductivities of the
cations and anions, respectively.
In Figures 7 and 8, we have plotted the temperature depend-
ence of 7D/(7D  19D) for the concentrations x  1/8 and x  1/16,
which can be taken, in the most simple model, as a measure of
the cationic transport number t.[22] It must be stressed that
these transport numbers are practically temperature independ-
ent; for x 1/8, we obtain t  0.18...0.25, and for x 1/16, t 
0.3...0.18. The size of the anion is thus not relevant for t values;
for instance at 360 K, t  0.25 and x 1/8 for both the
smallest and largest anion (1,1  and 4,4  ). At the concen-
tration x  1/16, the t value which corresponds to the sample
with the unsymmetrical anion 1,4  is close to 0.3 and is
identical to that of Li(TFSI) (1,1  ) at the same concentration to
corroborate the remark made above.
Figure 7. Temperature dependence of the ratio t  7D/(7D  19D), for the
compounds 1,1  , 3,3 , and 1,4  at x 1/8.
Figure 8. Temperature dependence of the ratio t  7D/(7D  19D) for the
compounds 1,1  , 2,2 , 3,3 , and 1,4  at x 1/16.
To compare the validity of the Nernst ± Einstein equation in
these compounds, we also have calculated per Equation (2) the
conductivity c as a function of the number N of cations per cm3,
N  NA/(MEO ¥ x1 Msalt), where NA is the Avogadro constant,
density  1.3 gcm3, and MEO and Msalt are the molar masses of
the PEO repeat unit and salt, respectively. The Haven ratio,
indicative of the jump mechanism, is taken to be unity for simple
interstitial diffusion and does not appear.
In Figure 10, we have plotted the measured (m) and the
calculated conductivity (c) versus temperature at concentration
x  1/16 for two compounds: one with the anion 2,2  and one
with the unsymmetrical anion 1,4 . One can see that the
agreement is excellent for both samples, again a proof for total
dissociation. The idea of full charge separation by intercalation of
one layer of polymer oxygen between Li and the anion in a
medium with such a low dielectric constant ( 5) at high
concentration has been claimed to be highly improbable for
many years; yet, the emergence of the highly delocalized anions
of the imide type have changed this conventional wisdom.
Recently, the crystal structure of P(EO)6LiAsF6
¹ WILEY-VCH-Verlag GmbH, 69451 Weinheim, Germany, 2002
1439-4235/02/03/07 $ 20.00+.50/0
unique coordination geometry in which two PEO chains folded
in complementary hemicylinders form a tube, in which the
nested Li ions are completely isolated from the intertubular
6 anions. This simple anion is probably, with its homologues
6, the closest in terms of extended charge
delocalization found in the imide family but has a limited
stability, precluding its use notwithstanding its toxicity. Whether
this type of coordination by two chains is maintained in the
amorphous state has probably been seen with 1,1  ,[3] and is
supported by molecular modeling.
This study on the homologues of TFSI has shown that the
conductivity is mainly controlled by similar laws, and most likely
the same phase diagram irrespective of anion size. The notion
that these anions are fully dissociated is corroborated by the
NMR results and the agreement between the calculated and
measured conductivities from the Nernst± Einstein equation. Up
to now, increasing the size of the anion has been associated with
an increase in conductivity, TFSI BF
size of an imide anion beyond that of TFSI (1,1 ) could have
been seen in terms of an increase of the free volume, with
dangling chain ends having a plasticizing effect. What we
observe is a decrease of the total conductivity The nonpolar
character of the perfluoroalkyl chains results in less available
space for conduction while their rigidity slows the segmental
motion. This is especially apparent for the 4,4  compound,
which is almost five times less conductive than TFSI. Yet, the
decrease in conductivity is not due to the slowing of the motion
of the anion only, as the relative mobilities of the positive and
negative charges (t) remain unchanged. With more than 250 ä3,
4,4  is more than 200 times more voluminous than Li (1.15 ä3);
however, it moves two times faster. In this respect, polymer
electrolytes differ completely from aqueous electrolytes, where
mobilities are inversely proportional to the size of the ion and its
solvation shell. The inclusion of the 1,4  anion in this study was
interesting, as its lack of symmetry could have induced specific
properties. Indeed, the conductivity values are those of the
average chain length or, even better, closer to 2,2  than to
3,3  compounds, probably as the anisotropy of the molecule
makes it easier to thread its way between chains.
This study provides a clearer picture on the influence of the
nature of the salts on conductivity and transport numbers. Yet,
despite the striking agreement with the Nernst± Einstein
equation and the quasisystematic observation of Arrhenius-type
temperature dependence of diffusion, we should not overlook
the fact that the measured conductivity obeys a free-volume
behavior. The discrepancy is apparent in Figure 9 at the upper
and lower end of the temperature span. This question remains
one of the many unsolved in the field of polymer electrolytes as a
telltale for other soft-matter media as well.
Sample preparation: LiTFSI (1,1 ) is commercially available from
Fluka (CH). Salts 2,2 , 3,3 , and 1,4  were donated by 3M
corporation (St. Paul, MN, USA). The 4,4 salt was prepared by
reaction of commercially available (Aldrich) perfluorobutanesulfon-
ylfluoride on lithium nitride in DMF according to Equation (3).
The salt was purified by recrystallization of the sparingly soluble
potassium salt in a water/ethanol mixture and treated with a
stoichiometric amount of LiCl in THF (KCl  ) to afford pure 4,4 .
The PEO complexes were made by the conventional solvent casting
105 gmol1 (Mw/Mn 2.2, Aldrich Europe) and salt corresponding to
the desired concentrations. Free-standing films were obtained after
evaporation of the degassed solutions in glass rings on polished
PTFE supports in a closed-loop argon circulation apparatus with
solvent trapping by 4x molecular sieves, housed inside a dry box. All
subsequent handling was done inside the dry box (O2, H2O  1 vpm).
For conductivity measurements, the
17 mm discs and mounted between two stainless-steel plunger
electrodes held in a KEL-F die and kept under spring pressure. The
system was housed in a gas-tight glass tube with electrical
feedthroughs and kept under dynamic vacuum. Temperature was
regulated to 1 K with a B¸chi TO-50 oven between 25 and 100C
and with a Haake cryostat below ambient temperatures. The
impedance measurement was automated with a Solartron 1260
analyzer ± computer interface in the frequency range from 5 to
13 MHz. Measurements were performed during both upward and
downward temperature ramps from 20C to 100C at 5 K intervals.
The time interval between measurements was controlled by the
reproducibility of the Nyquist diagram. The conductivity was
calculated from the actual geometrical factor. The thickness was

Calculations of the Rate Constants for
the Hydrogen Abstraction Reactions
Gui-xia Liu, Ze-sheng Li,* Jing-fa Xiao, Jing-yao Liu,
Qiang Fu, Xu-ri Huang, Chia-chung Sun, and
potential-energy surface ¥ variational transition state theory
The vinyl radical (C2H3) is a key intermediate in hydrocarbon
combustion reactions. A number of theoretical and experimental
investigations have been performed on the reactions of C2H3
with atoms, radicals, and molecules, including those with H2,
C2H2, CH3, CO, O2, NO, O, F, Cl, and so on.[1±11] It is known that
methane (CH4) and ethane (C2H6) are not only important
interstellar molecules, but are also essential constituents of
natural gas, so they participate widely in combustion processes.
Thus, the reaction of C2H3 with CH4 (or C2H6) might influence the
decay rate of the C2H3 radical, which might have a further effect
on the other chain reactions considered in combustion proc-
To the best of our knowledge, there are few experimental and
theoretical studies on the reactions between C2H3 and CH4 or
C2H6, though knowledge about their kinetic mechanisms may be
important for understanding combustion processes involving
In this paper, the direct hydrogen abstraction reactions of C2H3
with CH4 and C2H6 are investigated, and the rate constants over
the temperature range 200 ± 3000 K, calculated at the G2//
MP2(full)/6-31  G(d) level using canonical variational transition
state theory (CVT), are presented. For comparison with the TST
results at the G2//MP2(full)/6-31  G(d) level, the TST rate
constant calculations were also performed at the G2//QCISD/6-
31G(d,p) level. All the results calculated for the title reactions
may provide useful data for comparison with future laboratory
The optimized geometric parameters of the reactants, prod-
ucts, and transition states (TS) for the reactions C2H3 
QCISD/6-31G(d,p) results are very close to each other, except
for the lengths of the (forming) C2H6 bond and the (breaking)
C7H6 bond in TSa, and the lengths of the C1C2 bonds, the
taken directly from the electrode assembly after perfect contact had
been achieved at the upper end of the temperature excursion.
NMR: The experiments were performed by means of a homemade
spectrometer at the Larmor frequency o in the interval 30± 260 MHz
and a superconducting coil to give a direct magnetic field of 6.6 T.
The magnetic-field gradient pulses were delivered by a Techron
power supply, the maximum constant current reached 60 A during
5 ms. Details of the diffusion coefficients measurements are given
elsewhere.[13] The usual pulsed magnetic field gradient technique
and a sequence described by Stejskal and Tanner were used.[16]
